references.notes: The verse refers to "المنون" (fate/death) and "الدهر" (time/fate) as powerful, unyielding cosmological forces, central to pre-Islamic fatalism.
The verse mentions "المنيّة" (death/fate) as an unstoppable force, reflecting a pre-Islamic understanding of destiny.
The mention of "تميمة" (amulet) refers to a physical object used in pre-Islamic protective rituals, which were believed to ward off harm but are ineffective against fate.
The phrase "ريب الدهر" (the vicissitudes of time/fate) highlights the pre-Islamic concept of dahr as an unpredictable and often malevolent force.
This verse refers to "الزمان وريبه" (time and its vicissitudes) as a cause of sorrow, aligning with the pre-Islamic fatalistic view of time.
The verse emphasizes the destructive power of "الدهر" (time/fate), a key cosmological concept in pre-Islamic thought.
This verse reiterates the theme of "الدهر" (time/fate) as an inescapable force that brings about destruction.
The term "شؤم" (ill-omen) explicitly refers to a belief in negative signs or portents, a common aspect of pre-Islamic divination.
This verse describes the practice of "يسر" (casting lots) with "قداح" (arrows), a well-known pre-Islamic method of divination or distribution.
The mention of "العيوق" (Capella) and its observation by a "watcher" suggests pre-Islamic astral belief, where stars were often seen as significant or influential.
The act of "يعوذ بالأرطى" (seeking refuge in the Arṭā tree) reflects a pre-Islamic practice of venerating or seeking protection from sacred trees, often associated with jinn or spirits.
The verse again highlights "الدهر" (time/fate) as an overwhelming force that no one can withstand, a recurring pre-Islamic cosmological theme.
The verse refers to 'al-dahr' (time/fate) as the ultimate reality, reflecting the pre-Islamic fatalistic worldview where dahr is an impersonal, controlling force.
The term 'ḥarām' signifies a sacred prohibition or inviolability, reflecting pre-Islamic concepts of sacredness and the protection of a neighbor (jiwār) as a quasi-sacred duty.
The term 'al-ṭurrāq' refers to practitioners of ṭarq, a pre-Islamic form of divination, explicitly indicating a belief in omens and their interpretation.
The word 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) is used here as an irresistible, personified force, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of fate.
This verse describes the act of burial, specifically lowering the deceased into a 'deep pit' and 'the grave' (al-laḥd), which falls under pre-Islamic mortuary practices.
This verse contains the oath formula 'By your life', a common pre-Islamic expression used to swear by the life of the addressee.
The term 'manāyā' refers to the pre-Islamic concept of fate or destiny as an active, often personified, force that brings about death.
Dhū al-Majāz was a significant pre-Islamic market and pilgrimage site near Mecca, indicating a sacred geography.
The term 'ḥijja' explicitly refers to the pre-Islamic pilgrimage ritual, a central practice in Arabian religion.
This verse mentions Jamʿ (Muzdalifah) and Minā, both key locations within the pre-Islamic pilgrimage circuit.
The phrase 'عَمري' (by my life) is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, invoking one's own existence as a sacred entity for swearing.
The term 'al-hadīy' refers to an animal designated for sacrifice, a central practice in pre-Islamic Arabian rituals, especially pilgrimage, and its decoration is part of the ritual preparation.
The mention of 'ʿawdh' (amulet) indicates a belief in protective charms, which were commonly used in pre-Islamic Arabia to ward off evil or supernatural influences like jinn.
The term 'ʿukūf' denotes a state of ritual devotion or staying at a sacred site, a practice associated with pre-Islamic veneration of idols or sanctuaries.
The verse refers to al-ʿAyyūq (Aldebaran), a specific star used as a time marker, which reflects pre-Islamic astral observation and cosmological understanding of celestial bodies.
The phrase 'zajarta lahā ṭayra al-sanīḥ' refers to the pre-Islamic practice of augury, specifically interpreting omens from the flight of birds.
The term 'ḥarām' signifies a religious prohibition or sacred inviolability, indicating a pre-Islamic concept of taboo or forbidden action.
The phrase 'asbāb al-manīya' refers to the inescapable forces of fate or destiny, a central cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This verse explicitly mentions 'qird' and 'kāhil', which refer to specific objects used in pre-Islamic divination practices, particularly scapulimancy.
The mention of 'nawāʾiḥ' (professional wailing women) and 'al-bukā' (wailing) explicitly refers to pre-Islamic mourning rites and practices associated with death.
This verse compares the sound of a camel to the chanting of 'the one of the ancient law/ritual,' which refers to a pre-Islamic figure who performs sacred chants, indicating a sacral role.
This verse refers to 'fates' (al-manāyā) as an overpowering force ('ghālibāt'), reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of destiny.
The mention of 'ruqya' (incantation or charm) refers to a specific performative ritual practice common in pre-Islamic Arabian belief for protection or healing.
The verse mentions 'tamāʾim' (amulets), which were worn in pre-Islamic Arabia for protection against supernatural threats like the evil eye or jinn.
The verses describe an eagle's flight being interpreted as an omen, with the explicit mention of 'taʿīf' (augury), a pre-Islamic practice of divination by birds.
The speaker refers to 'heavy birds' as bringing either good news or fear, indicating their role as omens in pre-Islamic augury.
The term 'al-manāyā' refers to fate or destiny as an overarching, irresistible cosmological force in pre-Islamic belief.
The verse explicitly states that 'al-ʿiqbān' (eagles) provided information, and the speaker would have understood it 'if I augured' (aʿīf), confirming the practice of bird divination.
The phrase "وَاللَهِ" is an explicit oath invoking Allah, a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia.
"الأَقاديحُ" refers to arrows or lots used for divination (azlām), a well-documented pre-Islamic practice.
The phrase "وَاللَهِ" is an explicit oath invoking Allah, a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The term "كاهِلٍ" (kāhin) refers to a pre-Islamic diviner or priest, indicating a specific sacral role.
"أَصداءُ القُبورِ" alludes to the pre-Islamic belief in the ṣadā or hāma, a bird-like entity believed to emerge from the skull of the deceased and inhabit the grave.
This verse describes the daughters performing specific mourning rites, including unveiling and striking their chests with sandals, which are characteristic of pre-Islamic lamentation.
This verse explicitly refers to the speaker being shrouded in burial cloths and having his arm as a pillow, detailing pre-Islamic burial practices.
This verse contains the oath formula 'تَاللَهِ' (By God), which is a common pre-Islamic Arabian oath invoking a deity.
The verse attributes the causation of wind to 'al-Naʿāmā', a specific star or asterism, indicating a belief in astral influence on natural phenomena.
The term 'الكاهِلِيَّةِ' (al-Kāhiliyya) refers to the dwellings or people associated with a kāhin, a pre-Islamic diviner or priest, thus referencing a tribal sacral role.
This verse contains the oath formula 'لَعَمري' (by my life), which is a common pre-Islamic oath invoking one's life as a sacred entity.
The term 'رَجَبِيَّة' refers to the month of Rajab, a sacred month in pre-Islamic Arabia, indicating a connection to the pre-Islamic sacred calendar.
The phrase 'يُنشَرَ في القَتلى' (resurrected among the dead) refers to a conceptual belief about the state of the deceased and a hypothetical return, even if used hyperbolically.
The verse explicitly mentions 'ḥajj' (pilgrimage), which was a central pre-Islamic ritual in Arabia.
This verse refers to the pre-Islamic belief in the *hama* bird (هامُها), which was thought to emerge from the skull of the deceased and represent their soul.
The phrase "وَقاسَمَها بِاللَهِ" constitutes an oath sworn by Allah, a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia where Allah was recognized as a high god.
This verse explicitly mentions 'al-zalam,' referring to the arrows used for divination (azlām) in pre-Islamic Arabian practices.
